data.title: South Korea’s Largest Internet Portal Naver Plans Merger with Dunamu to Launch Won-Backed Stablecoin and Crypto Services
data.description:
Naver Moves to Acquire Dunamu in Major South Korean Fintech Shake-Up

South Korea’s leading internet portal, Naver Corp., is set to confirm plans to acquire Dunamu Inc., the operator of cryptocurrency exchange Upbit, at its board meeting next week, according to industry sources.

Dunamu is scheduled to hold a board meeting on 26 November to finalise the details of the proposal.

Dunamu and Naver Financial are expected to hold separate board meetings on 26 November and the potential merger of the two companies is on the agenda. https://t.co/kRlCGShCOo

— Douglas Kim (@douglaskimkorea) November 19, 2025

The acquisition would see Naver expand its footprint in digital finance, leveraging Dunamu’s status as South Korea’s largest crypto exchange and the world’s fourth-largest by trading volume.

FYI Naver buying Dunamu in Korea would be the equivalent of Google buying Coinbase in the US https://t.co/0zJGPeP6Y4

— Jeff Park (@dgt10011) November 19, 2025

How Will the Stock Swap Work

The acquisition is expected to take the form of a comprehensive stock swap through Naver’s fintech arm, Naver Financial.

Under the proposed terms, one Dunamu share would be exchanged for three Naver Financial shares, effectively making Dunamu a wholly owned subsidiary.

The deal would position Dunamu Chairman Song Chi-hyung as the largest shareholder of Naver Financial, with Naver becoming the second-largest shareholder.

Dunamu Chairman Song Chi-hyung is the Founder and Chairman of Dunamu, the South Korean fintech company that operates the nation's largest cryptocurrency exchange, Upbit.

Naver Financial currently has a corporate value estimated between 4.7 trillion and 5 trillion won, while Dunamu’s value is estimated at 14 trillion to 15 trillion won.

Mirae Asset Group, which holds a 30% stake in Naver Financial, has reportedly agreed in principle to the merger despite viewing Naver Financial as undervalued relative to Dunamu.

The merger will require approval from two-thirds of shareholders at a special meeting and consent from at least one-third of total issued shares.

Regulatory Questions and Market Implications

The deal initially raised concerns about Korea’s “separation of finance and cryptocurrency” regulations.

Industry sources indicate, however, that regulators consider Naver Financial a big-tech fintech operator rather than a conventional financial institution, allowing the merger to move forward under existing laws.

The Fair Trade Commission is expected to review potential market dominance issues once shareholder approval is secured.

If completed, the merger could reshape South Korea’s digital finance sector.

Naver Pay’s extensive payment network would integrate with Upbit’s crypto trading and blockchain infrastructure, potentially creating a single platform spanning payments, digital asset management, investments, and crypto custody services.

The two firms are also expected to launch a won-backed stablecoin project and other digital finance initiatives, signalling ambitions to expand beyond traditional fintech.

⚡️Naver is set to acquire Upbit parent Dunamu via full equity swap, with a South Korean won stablecoin in view. pic.twitter.com/pI9oz2jnj9

— MonkeyPuppet (@MonkeyPupp) November 19, 2025

Who Will Hold Control After the Merger

Assuming the proposed 1-to-3 exchange ratio, Dunamu Chairman Song would hold around 19% of Naver Financial shares, while Vice Chairman Kim Hyeong-nyeon would control approximately 9%.

Naver, which currently owns 70% of Naver Financial, would see its stake drop to roughly 17%, becoming the second-largest shareholder.

This structure positions Dunamu’s management as the dominant voice in the combined entity, while Naver retains a significant influence.

The Future of Digital Finance in Korea

Coinlive views the merger as a sign that the line between traditional tech platforms and cryptocurrency services is increasingly blurred in South Korea.

By integrating payments, trading, and blockchain capabilities under one roof, Naver and Dunamu are laying the foundation for a comprehensive digital finance ecosystem.

The question for investors and regulators alike is whether such consolidation will accelerate innovation or concentrate influence in the hands of a few, potentially reshaping the competitive landscape for years to come.

data.title: XRP Ledger May Shift Toward Reward-Based Participation as Ripple Considers Staking Features for Long-Term Users
data.description:
Ripple Considers Bringing Staking To XRP Ledger To Strengthen Security And Incentives

Ripple is exploring the possibility of introducing native staking to the XRP Ledger (XRPL), a move that could expand the blockchain’s role in decentralized finance and reshape participation incentives for validators and token holders.

The discussion gained momentum after J. Ayo Akinyele, head of engineering at RippleX, highlighted the potential benefits of staking in a recent blog post.

Could Staking Encourage Long-Term Participation On XRPL

Akinyele explained that staking “can encourage long-term participation and provide rewards to participants who help maintain consensus, which could strengthen network security.”

He noted that enabling staking would require redesigning the ledger’s core structures, including the creation of a source for staking rewards and a fair distribution method.

Currently, XRPL maintains a deflationary model by burning transaction fees, and redirecting these as rewards would involve significant architectural changes.

The XRP Ledger was originally designed for efficient cross-border payments and value transfer, its primary use case for years.

4/6 Most networks today use staking to align incentives, but XRP is different:
* Transaction fees are burned
* It’s built for settlement of any asset quickly, efficiently and at low cost
* Network validators have an equal vote regardless of assets held – stability and trust, not…

— J. Ayo Akinyele (@ja_akinyele) November 18, 2025

With XRP gaining adoption in corporate treasuries and exchange-traded products, Ripple is exploring whether staking could help maintain the network’s competitive edge while expanding utility beyond traditional payments.

How Would Staking Work On XRPL

David Schwartz, Ripple’s chief technology officer, outlined two conceptual approaches to integrate staking.

The first proposes a dual-layer consensus model.

An “inner” layer of approximately 16 validators, selected by the broader validator set based on stake, would manage ledger advancement using staking and slashing mechanisms to prevent protocol violations.

The “outer” layer, composed of existing validators, would oversee governance, amendments, and fees.

The second approach would maintain XRPL’s current consensus system while redirecting transaction fees to fund zero-knowledge proof (ZKP) verification.

ZKPs allow participants to validate actions without revealing sensitive information, offering a trust-minimised method of verification.

There are two ideas floating around. Both are awesome technically but probably not realistically likely to be good, at least not any time soon.

One is to switch to a two-layer consensus model with the inner layer being incentivized. The inner layer would have 16 inner validators…

— David 'JoelKatz' Schwartz (@JoelKatz) November 18, 2025

Schwartz emphasised that while both models are “technically promising,” they are “not practical” in the near term due to high complexity, engineering effort, and potential risks.

Ripple Engages With US Fed Proposal To Expand Crypto Access

Ripple’s exploration of staking coincides with recent developments in the US regulatory environment.

Earlier this month, Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller proposed allowing crypto firms access to “skinny” Fed accounts, enabling direct interaction with the central bank’s payment systems.

Stu Alderoty, Ripple’s chief legal officer, described the proposal as potentially transformative, saying it could speed up settlement, lower costs, and support Ripple’s RLUSD stablecoin in a competitive market dominated by Tether and Circle.

Alderoty noted that direct access to the Fed would enhance stability and redeemability for digital assets, reducing reliance on traditional banks that often hesitate to service crypto firms.

#Ripple (Alderoty) comments on the Fed "skinny" account proposal saying it might assuage bank concerns but reiterates the superiority of a master account. (Skinny accounts would allow reserve redemptions w/out benefits like interest, overdrafts & emergency lending access.) #RLUSDpic.twitter.com/0nQqlQBsCf

— WrathofKahneman (@WKahneman) November 7, 2025

Ripple, which previously applied for a Fed master account for RLUSD, views the proposal as a significant opportunity to integrate crypto more deeply into mainstream financial infrastructure.

Staking Could Reshape XRP’s Role In DeFi

The possibility of staking on XRPL raises questions about the ledger’s foundational design, particularly its Proof of Association model, which prioritises trust and reliability over economic incentives.

🚨BREAKING: Ripple CTO Says XRPL May Need “A Fresh Look” — Even Floating the Idea of Native Staking for the First Time Ever 💥

So here’s what just happened — @Ripple CTO David Schwartz (@JoelKatz) shook the entire $XRP community by saying something nobody expected:

The $XRP… pic.twitter.com/GN2bbVJsl1

— Diana (@InvestWithD) November 19, 2025

Akinyele and Schwartz emphasised that while the concept could boost security and long-term participation, any implementation would require careful consideration of protocol principles and network stability.

Internal discussions at Ripple continue as the team weighs whether staking could enhance XRPL’s utility without compromising its existing consensus system.

The dialogue reflects a broader effort to position XRP within the growing DeFi ecosystem while preserving the network’s efficiency and trust-based architecture.

data.title: Deconstructing DAT: Building Deep Analytics Beyond mNAV
data.description:
Author: kokii.eth Source: X, @kokii_eth

Abstract

  • The 80/20 split: The DAT industry exhibits a power-law distribution, with leading projects in each category occupying the vast majority of the market share, while long-tail projects struggle to survive. Despite the existence of a bubble, DAT based on real assets and differentiated financial strategies still represents significant financial innovation.

  • Value and sentiment divergence: mNAV often obscures long-term value drivers. Our growth driver decomposition model separates fundamental compound growth from market sentiment. Data shows that companies like BMNR and HSDT have seen continuous growth in per-share value, while the decline in most DAT stock prices is mainly due to sentiment contraction, not fundamental deterioration.

  • Value and sentiment divergence: mNAV often obscures long-term value drivers.

  • The divergence between value and sentiment ...>

    • DAT Company Market Capitalization Growth Trend (Based on 30 Tracked Targets)

    DAT is essentially a publicly traded stock, and its evaluation needs to focus on two main factors:

    • Company Value (NAV/Net Asset Value): Reflects the company's true value. For DAT, this refers to the total current assets held on the balance sheet—including digital assets and undeployed cash equivalents. The core value driver of the company is not traditional operating profit, but rather the holding and growth of digital assets.

      DAT is essentially a publicly traded stock, and its evaluation needs to focus on two main factors:

      • Company Value (NAV): Reflects the company's true value. For DAT, this refers to the total current assets held on the balance sheet—including digital assets and undeployed cash equivalents.

        • NAV Multiple / Multiple NAV (mNAV)

        While NAV reflects a company's underlying assets, it does not reflect the market's assessment of those assets. This is where market capitalization comes in: the market's real-time assessment of a company's value.

        ... Market Cap and NAV Relationship: Provides the most watched metric in the DAT space: mNAV (NAV Multiple)

        mNAV represents how much the market is willing to pay for each dollar of net asset value:

        • mNAV > 1 → Indicates that the market is optimistic about the company's prospects or believes the company has growth potential. The market values ​​the company higher than its assets on its balance sheet, typically taking into account expected future growth per token.

          mNAV > 1 → Indicates that the market is optimistic about the company's prospects or believes the company has growth potential. The market values ​​the company higher than its assets on its balance sheet, usually taking into account expected future growth per token.

          mNAV < 1 → Reflects market skepticism. Investors may worry about equity dilution, question management discipline, or believe that the company's digital asset exposure is failing to effectively translate into shareholder value. Essentially, mNAV is a sentiment multiplier built on fundamentals, revealing the market's belief in DAT's ability to accumulate digital asset holdings.

          BTC DATs' mNAV Multiples (excluding CLSK, CORZ, NAKA, and SGNS)

          As of today, in the BTC DAT category, Strategy (MSTR), GME, and MARA are all close to 1.0 after the recent market correction. However, most other BTC DATs have mNAVs below 1.0, with EMPD having the lowest at around 0.5.

          Newer DATs such as DJT and USBC currently have mNAVs of around 2-3, reflecting the speculative nature of early DATs.

          A few exceptions: CLSK is around 4 and CORZ is close to 7, both AI data center companies (formerly BTC miners), indicating that specific narratives or structural factors are still driving premiums despite overall market normalization. The ETH DAT market is similar: BMNR, SBET, and GAME are trading around 1x mNAV, reflecting fair value pricing; BTBT and COSM have higher multiples because these companies have profitable business lines beyond digital asset holdings, and the market may not consider them purely DAT-based valuations. Among the Solana DATs registered with PIPE shares, only HSDT is trading at a slight premium of 1.12x (as of November 12, 2025), while the rest are slightly below 1, indicating that market movements are largely consistent with fundamentals and have cooled down from earlier cycles. Premiums and Discounts Premiums/discounts are essentially another representation of mNAV, measuring the market's confidence in or speculation about a company's treasury value, expressed as a relative price rather than a multiple. High premiums indicate leverage, strong sentiment, or excess operating returns, while discounts typically reflect concerns about equity dilution or weak capital discipline. The data dashboard reveals extreme premium cases of approximately 800% for companies like COSM and CORZ, largely due to market valuations based on their existing core businesses, rather than their DAT (Data, Aid, and Technology) attributes. Assessing DAT's intrinsic growth requires tracking both digital asset holdings and the number of outstanding shares. A healthy DAT aims for growth in two metrics: increasing digital asset holdings to expand the underlying asset base and raising funds through new share issuances to support growth. While new share issuances dilute existing shareholders' equity, this dilution can be beneficial if asset growth outpaces new share issuance. A key derivative metric, digital asset per share, measures how much digital asset is effectively represented per share, reflecting the extent of shareholder exposure amplification. An increase in digital asset per share indicates that proceeds from issuance are used for asset growth rather than offsetting equity dilution. Of the 30 DATs tracked, few have managed to grow their digital assets per share along a steady upward trend. Notable exceptions include Strategy (MSTR), BMNR, HSDT, ETHM, BTCS, CEP, and UPXI. Experience shows that many DATs, even those with strong early performance, experience sharp equity dilution due to large-scale new share issuances. In contrast, the aforementioned companies have maintained consistent growth without significant declines, indicating a more prudent strategy in balancing capital issuance and asset accumulation.

          ETH DATs with continuously increasing assets per share: BMNR, ETHM, BTCS

          SOL DATs with continuously increasing assets per share: HSDT, UPXI

          Other market indicators

          BTC DATs Trading Volume (USD) Market Share

          ETH DATs Crypto Asset Holding Market Share

          In the ETH DAT sector, Bitmine also dominates: holding over 66% of the total ETH DAT holdings (approximately 2.9% of the ETH supply), accounting for...

          Solana DAT has a 68% market capitalization and 85% trading volume. SBET, the second largest player, holds approximately 16-20% of ETH's holdings and market capitalization, while BTBT ranks third (approximately 6%). The Solana DAT market has relatively low concentration: FORD leads with 45% market capitalization and 44% SOL holdings. HSDT, DFDV, STSS, and UPXI each hold approximately 13-14% of the holdings, but Solana Company (HSDT) leads its peers with approximately 22% market capitalization.

          SOL DATs Crypto Asset Holding Market Share

          SOL DATs Market Cap Market Share

          Interestingly, in terms of trading volume, the situation is the opposite: DFDV and UPXI are more active than FORD.

          SOL DATs Market Cap Market Share

          Interestingly, in terms of trading volume, the situation is the opposite: DFDV and UPXI are more active than FORD.

          ... Historical trends indicate that these two companies were pioneers in the Solana DAT category, and this advantage seems to continue to this day. Even with FORD achieving a higher NAV later, the trading momentum and market attention maintained by early entrants remain difficult to shake.

          SOL DATs Trading Volume (USD) Market Share

          2. Limitations and Misconceptions

          While the definitions are simple, tracking these basic metrics is not easy—mainly because the data in the SEC filings is neither real-time nor as standardized as on-chain data.

          The best source for balance sheet accounting format is the 10-Q form, but it's only published quarterly. Many companies use custom-designed or branded PDFs, making extraction more difficult. Even when data is consistently reported in the same format, it's often embedded in text files requiring semantic parsing. Furthermore, the formatting of reporting items varies from company to company, which is understandable given differences in equity structures and financial assets. Sources for holdings updates can be highly fragmented—some companies don't even file with the SEC, instead disclosing changes via Twitter, press releases, or media interviews. Nevertheless, most stock market metrics (such as price and volume) are fairly standardized. However, the number of outstanding shares remains difficult to track—companies don't need to report daily through filings, and many data dashboards rely on third-party APIs that retrieve data from market makers or banks, often with delays of several days. One best practice comes from Bitmine, which reports its digital asset holdings weekly (sometimes more frequently) via 8-K filings. When interpreting DAT data, be aware of how these data challenges can distort metrics: Low-frequency (monthly/quarterly) updates cause NAV to become outdated, inflating mNAV or premiums. Some DATs hold DeFi tokens, NFTs, other stocks, or semi-liquid assets, complicating asset valuation. Share updates: Failure to file large-scale issuance or buyback declarations will affect estimated market capitalization, mNAV, premium/discount, and digital assets per share.

        We have identified some common blind spots in public reporting:

        • Pro-Forma Accounting: Most data dashboards rely solely on the number of shares outstanding and do not account for the potential exercise of previously issued warrants. In DAT's PIPE trading, warrants are typically bundled with PIPE stock, and the exercise price is usually equal to or higher than the PIPE stock price. The warrants can be exercised at any time after the exercise date, as long as the stock price is above that level—a reasonable move for the holder. Because exercised warrants increase the number of outstanding shares but not necessarily their value, they have a significant dilutive effect on key metrics. Including these unexercised warrants in simulations can more accurately reflect the potential dilutive effect and the true risk exposure of shareholders.

          • Prefunded Warrants: The proceeds from these warrants have been received and included in NAV, but the corresponding shares have not yet been issued. In many cases, the exercise price of these warrants is close to zero, meaning that once exercised, the number of shares will increase without generating new proceeds—the dilution effect on equity is one-sided. We believe these warrants should be included in outstanding shares; otherwise, the resulting mNAV calculation will underestimate market capitalization and overestimate NAV, creating an imbalance. Pending Mergers and Acquisitions and PIPEs: When a company announces a new PIPE, the cash proceeds are typically reflected in the NAV update before the shares are formally issued through the S-3 filing. If no pro forma adjustment is made to the shares, the denominator of the NAV per share is underestimated, artificially inflating the metric. The following chart summarizes the main types of share issuance plans and their impact on outstanding shares.

          Debt Data and Derivatives Exposure: Apart from Artemis, almost no data dashboards currently include information on debt liabilities or leverage exposure. This omission distorts NAV, especially for DATs employing structured income or collateralized strategies.

          After considering debt, adjusted NAV (and adjusted mNAV) should reflect true book value. This allows for a clear comparison between pure treasury exposure DATs (such as MSTR) and hybrid operating DATs (such as BMNR or SBET). What role does debt play in DAT management? In traditional finance, companies issue debt to finance growth while protecting shareholder ownership.

      • Growth Drivers and Underlying Price

      If we view DAT's stock price as the product of several potential growth factors (token growth per share, token price, and market sentiment), we can break it down to see what truly drives performance, rather than just the narrative.

      ... Formally, we can represent the stock price at time t as: This decomposition method allows us to isolate each factor and independently track the factors that truly drive price fluctuations: When the stock price falls, we can examine whether it's due to cooling market sentiment, a decline in the price of the underlying asset, or a deterioration in the company's fundamentals—and conversely, which of these factors drives the stock price upward. It also helps us see through noise—for example, when the company's intrinsic value continues to grow while the market price falls. When we break down the price growth of Bitmine (BMNR), we find that since its launch, each ETH has steadily increased, while mNAV (sentiment multiple) has contracted significantly. This indicates that its fundamentals remain strong, with only the hype in the market cooling down. Summarizing this framework into three growth factors, we can chart DAT companies by category to assess their overall health: BTC DAT: Most fundamental value growth is relatively stable, such as MSTR, CLSK, and CEP, showing a clear upward trend. In contrast, while the fundamentals of SMLR, FLD, DJT, LMFA, and EMPD remained stable, market sentiment for these companies has plummeted since the tracking began, which is the main reason for their stock price declines. Currently, the only DAT experiencing a decline in real value is SQNS.

      ETH DAT: As category forerunners, ETHZ and SBET benefited from the initial surge in market sentiment, although their per-ETH value remained relatively stable. Subsequently, BMNR, ETHM, BTCS, BTBT, and GAME have all seen steady growth in per-share value, although their mNAV growth has been trending downwards—potentially indicating they launched near the top of a market cycle.

      ... FGNX was an exception, experiencing severe equity dilution and a sharp decline in market sentiment, resulting in significantly weaker-than-expected performance. SOL DAT: HSDT saw the most significant increase in SOL per share, tripling from October to the time of the report's release; UPXI also saw steady growth, but on a smaller scale. DFDV benefited from improved market sentiment, but its SOL per share declined over the same period, indicating that the increase was more driven by market sentiment than fundamentals. Meanwhile, FORD and STSS both saw significant expansion in mNAV, but fundamental value growth remained largely flat, suggesting that performance was driven by market sentiment rather than balance sheet performance. As shown in the chart above, most DAT companies have experienced market cooling or contraction phases since their inception. To understand their potential trajectory, we can further reconstruct the theoretical fundamental price of each company—essentially answering: "If market conditions were the same as on the day DAT was launched, what would the stock price be today?" In other words, if you held one share of the stock from the company's inception and allowed it to gradually accumulate inventory and issue new shares over time, what would that share be worth today? The chart below shows that the fundamental value of several DAT companies—HSDT, BMNR, BTBT, BTCS, CORZ, and CEP—has steadily increased, but their stock prices have not fully reflected this due to changes in the market environment. These companies have seen significant growth in their fundamental metrics since their inception, even as overall market sentiment has contracted.

      Share Issuance and Dilution

data.title: From Nvidia to Binance, selling shovels is the strongest business model.
data.description: In 1849, the California Gold Rush saw countless people flocking to the American West, dreaming of striking it rich. German immigrant Levi Strauss initially wanted to join the gold rush, but he astutely recognized another business opportunity: miners' trousers were frequently worn out, and they desperately needed more durable work clothes. So, he made a batch of jeans out of canvas and sold them specifically to gold miners, thus giving birth to a clothing empire called "Levi's." Meanwhile, the vast majority of those who actually participated in the gold rush lost everything. On November 20, 2024, Nvidia once again delivered an "unbelievable" financial report. Q3 revenue hit a record $57 billion, a 62% year-over-year increase; net profit reached $31.9 billion, a staggering 65% year-over-year surge. The latest generation of GPUs remains a scarce commodity, "money can't always buy," with the entire AI industry working for its development. Meanwhile, in the cryptocurrency world across the cyberspace, the same scenario is playing out. From the 2017 ICO bull market to the 2020 DeFi summer, and then to the Bitcoin ETF and Meme wave in 2024, in each narrative and each wave of get-rich-quick stories, retail investors, project teams, and VCs constantly rotate, but only exchanges like Binance consistently remain at the top of the food chain. History always rhymes. From the California Gold Rush of 1849 to the cryptocurrency craze and the AI ​​wave, the biggest winners are often not the "gold miners" who directly compete, but those who provide them with the "shovels." "Selling shovels" is the strongest business model for navigating cycles and reaping uncertainties. The AI ​​Gold Rush: Nvidia's Fortunes In the public's perception, the protagonist of this AI wave is undoubtedly the large-scale model represented by ChatGPT—an intelligent agent capable of writing copy, drawing, and coding. However, from a business and profit perspective, the essence of this AI wave is not a "massive explosion of applications," but rather an unprecedented revolution in computing power. Just like the California Gold Rush of the 19th century, tech giants like Meta, Google, and Alibaba are all gold diggers, launching an AI gold rush. Meta recently announced a whopping $72 billion investment in AI infrastructure this year, and indicated that spending will be even higher next year. CEO Mark Zuckerberg stated that he would rather risk "missing out on hundreds of billions of dollars" than fall behind in the development of superintelligence. Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI, among others, have all invested record amounts of capital in AI. Tech giants are going crazy, and Jensen Huang is grinning from ear to ear; he's the Levi Strauss of the AI ​​era. Every company wanting to build a large-scale model has to purchase GPUs and rent GPU cloud services on a massive scale. Each model iteration consumes enormous amounts of training and inference resources. If the model can't compete with the competition, or the application can't find a clear commercialization path, it can be scrapped and started over, but the purchased GPUs and signed computing power contracts have already been paid for in real money. In other words, everyone is still exploring the questions of "Can AI change the world?" and "Can AI applications be profitable in the long run?", but if you want to participate in this game, you must first pay an "entry tax" to the computing power providers. Nvidia stands precisely at the very top of this computing power food chain. It virtually monopolizes the high-performance training chip market. The H100, H200, and B100 have become the "golden shovels" that AI companies vie for. It extends from GPUs downwards, connecting the software ecosystem (CUDA), development tools, and framework support, further forming a dual moat of technology and ecosystem. It doesn't need to bet on which large model will win; it only needs the entire industry to continuously "bet": betting that AI can create a certain future and support higher valuations and budgets. In the traditional internet era, Amazon's AWS played a similar role. Whether a startup survived was one thing, but sorry, you had to pay for cloud resources first. Of course, Nvidia isn't isolated; behind it is an entire "shovel-selling supply chain," and they are also big winners in the AI ​​wave. GPUs require high-speed interconnects and optical modules, and A-share listed companies like New E-Sun, InnoLight Technology, and Tianfu Communication have become indispensable parts of this "shovel," with their stock prices rising several times this year. Data center upgrades require a large number of server racks, power systems, and cooling solutions. From liquid cooling and power distribution to data center infrastructure, new industry opportunities are constantly emerging. Storage, PCBs, connectors, packaging and testing—all component manufacturers linked to "AI servers" are reaping valuations and profits in this wave. This is the terrifying aspect of the "selling shovels" model: Gold diggers may lose money, and the gold-digging may fail, but as long as people keep digging, those selling shovels will never lose money. While large-scale models are still struggling with "how to make money," the computing power and hardware supply chain is already steadily raking in profits. If Nvidia is the shovel seller for AI, then who is the shovel seller for Crypto? The answer is obvious: exchanges. The industry is constantly changing, but the only constant is that exchanges are always printing money. 2017 was the first truly global bull market in crypto history. The barrier to entry for issuing tokens is extremely low; a white paper and a few PowerPoint slides are all it takes to launch and raise funds. Investors frantically chase after "10x or 100x coins," resulting in countless tokens going public only to plummet to zero. Most projects are frozen or delisted within 1-2 years, with even the founding teams disappearing from the timeline. However, listing a token incurs fees, user transactions incur transaction fees, and futures contracts are charged based on position size. Cryptocurrency prices can plummet, but exchanges only need trading volume to make money; the more frequent the trading and the more volatile the market, the more they profit. In 2020, during the DeFi boom, Uniswap challenged traditional order books with its AMM model, and various mining, lending, and liquidity pools made it seem like "centralized exchanges were no longer needed." However, the reality is quite different. Large amounts of funds were withdrawn from CEXs to on-chain mining, and then returned to CEXs during peak periods and crashes for risk control, cashing out, and hedging. In terms of narrative, DeFi is the future, but CEXs remain the preferred entry point for deposits, withdrawals, hedging, and perpetual contract trading. By 2024–2025, Bitcoin ETFs, the Solana ecosystem, and Meme 2.0 once again propelled crypto to new heights. Throughout this cycle, regardless of whether the narrative shifts to "institutional entry" or "on-chain paradise," one fact remains unchanged: a large amount of capital seeking leverage continues to flow into centralized exchanges; leverage, futures, options, perpetual contracts, and various structured products constitute the "profit moat" of these exchanges. Furthermore, CEXs are also integrating with DEXs at the product level, making trading on-chain assets within CEXs a common practice. Cryptocurrency prices may fluctuate, projects may change, regulations may tighten, and sectors may shift, but as long as trading continues and volatility persists, exchanges remain the most stable "shovel sellers" in this game. Besides exchanges, there are many other "shovel sellers" in the crypto world: For example, mining machine companies like Bitmain profit by selling mining machines rather than mining, allowing them to remain profitable through multiple bull and bear market cycles. Infura, Alchemy, and others provide API services and benefit from the growth of blockchain applications; Tether, Circle, and other stablecoin issuers earn "seigniorage" from digital dollars through interest rate differentials and asset allocation; Pump.Fun and other asset issuance platforms continuously collect taxes by issuing meme assets in bulk. In these positions, they don't need to bet correctly on which chain will win or which meme will explode every time, but as long as speculation and liquidity exist, they can steadily print money. Why is "selling shovels" the most powerful business model? The real business world is far more brutal than people imagine; innovation is often a matter of life and death. Success requires not only personal effort but also reliance on historical trends. For any cyclical industry, the outcome is often like this: Building upper-level applications is like mining for gold, pursuing Alpha (excess returns). You need to bet on the right direction, the right timing, and beat your competitors. The win rate is extremely low, the odds are extremely high, and a slight misjudgment can lead to total loss. Building underlying infrastructure is like being an upstream "shovel seller," earning Beta. As long as the entire industry continues to grow and the number of players continues to increase, you can reap the benefits of scale and network effects. Shovel sellers are in a business of probability, not luck. Nvidia doesn't need to choose which AI model can "run," and Binance doesn't need to judge which narrative will last the longest. They only need one condition: "Everyone continues to play this game." Moreover, once you get used to Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem, the migration cost is unimaginably high. Once your assets are all on a large exchange and you're used to its depth and liquidity, it's very difficult to adapt to a smaller exchange. The ultimate outcome of the shovel-selling business is often monopoly. Once a monopoly is formed, pricing power is entirely in the hands of the shovel sellers; just look at Nvidia's gross profit margin of 73%. To summarize from a very blunt perspective: Companies that sell shovels earn "industry-existing taxes"; companies that mine for gold earn "time window dividends"—they must seize user mindshare within a short window, or they will be abandoned; those who create content or narratives earn "money from fluctuating attention"—once the trend shifts, the traffic evaporates instantly. To put it more bluntly: Selling shovels is betting that "this era will move down this path"; developing applications is betting that "everyone will only choose my company." The former is a macro-level proposition, the latter is a brutal elimination process. Therefore, from a probability perspective, the odds of winning by selling shovels are an order of magnitude higher. For retail investors and entrepreneurs like myself, this is also a profound lesson: If you can't see who the ultimate winner will be, or don't know which asset will continue to rise several times over, then invest in the person who delivers water to all the miners, sells shovels, or even just sells jeans. Finally, let me share some data: Ctrip's Q3 net profit was 19.919 billion yuan, surpassing Moutai (19.2 billion yuan) and Xiaomi (11.3 billion yuan). Don't just focus on who shines the brightest in the story; think about who can consistently generate revenue across all the stories. In times of frenzy, serving the frenzy, but remaining calm, is the highest wisdom in business.

data.title: Why is the final outcome outlook worse than the market expected?
data.description:
Author: arndxt Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Over the past few months, my stance has undergone a significant shift: from "extreme bearishness turning bullish" (general pessimism often sets the stage for a rebound) to "bearishness turning into genuine concern that the system is entering a more vulnerable phase."

This is not due to a single event, but rather a comprehensive consideration of five mutually reinforcing trends:

  1. The risk of policy missteps is rising. The Federal Reserve is still tightening financial conditions amid increased data uncertainty and clear signs of an economic slowdown.

  2. The cluster of artificial intelligence/mega-tech stocks is shifting from cash abundance to leverage-driven growth. This transforms the risk from simple stock volatility to a more typical credit cycle problem.

  3. Artificial intelligence/mega-tech stocks are shifting from cash abundance to leverage-driven growth. This shifts the risk from simple stock volatility to a more typical credit cycle problem.

    4. Private credit and loan valuations are beginning to diverge. Beneath the surface, early warning signs of model valuation pressures are emerging, albeit preliminary, but worrying. The K-shaped economy is solidifying into a political problem. A growing number of people believe the social contract is no longer credible, which will ultimately manifest in policy. Market concentration has become a systemic and political vulnerability. When approximately 40% of the index's weight is concentrated in the hands of a few geopolitically and leverage-sensitive tech giants, they cease to be merely growth stories and become targets of national security and policy intervention. The baseline scenario may still be that policymakers will ultimately "continue as before": inject liquidity back into the next political cycle to support asset prices. But the path to this outcome is more arduous, more credit-driven, and more politically uncertain than the standard "buy the dip" strategy envisions.

    1. Macroeconomic Stance

    For most of this cycle, a "bearish but constructive" stance is reasonable:

    • Inflation is high but continues to decline, but the growth rate is slowing down.

    • Policies generally remain supportive.

    • Risky assets are overvalued, but pullbacks are usually supported by liquidity. Several key factors have now changed: Government shutdown: We experienced a prolonged shutdown that disrupted the release schedule and quality of key macroeconomic data. **Statistical Statistical Uncertainty:** Senior officials themselves have acknowledged damage to federal statistical agencies, meaning the credibility of core data series anchored to trillions of dollars in assets has declined. **A Hawkish Shift Amid Economic Weakness:** Against this backdrop, the Federal Reserve continues to adopt a more hawkish stance on interest rate expectations and balance sheet policy, tightening financial conditions even as forward-looking indicators deteriorate. In other words, the system is tightening towards ambiguity and emerging pressures, rather than moving away from them. This is a drastically different risk landscape. 2. Policy Tightening Amidst the Fog The core concern is not policy tightening itself, but rather the areas and methods of tightening: Data Fog: Key data releases (inflation, employment) have been delayed, distorted, or questioned since the shutdown. The Fed's own "dashboard" has become unreliable at the most critical moment. Interest Rate Expectations: Although forward-looking indicators suggest inflation will continue to decline early next year, hawkish comments from Fed officials have reduced the market's implied probability of a short-term rate cut. Balance Sheet: The quantitative tightening process continues, and the tendency to transfer more duration risk to the private sector means that even if policy rates remain unchanged, this stance still inherently exerts hawkish pressure on financial conditions. Historically, the Federal Reserve's mistakes have often been timing errors: raising rates too late and lowering them too late. We risk repeating this: tightening policy amid slowing growth and uncertain data, rather than preemptively easing. 3. Artificial Intelligence and Mega Tech Stocks Become a Leveraged Growth Story The second structural shift lies in the characteristics of mega tech stocks and AI leaders: Over the past decade, the core seven tech giants have essentially been bond-like stocks: possessing dominant businesses, huge free cash flow, large-scale buybacks, and limited net leverage. In the past two to three years, this free cash flow has been increasingly invested in AI capital expenditures: data centers, chips, and infrastructure. Now we are entering a new phase where new AI capital expenditures increasingly rely on debt issuance rather than solely on internally generated cash. The impacts are as follows: Credit spreads and credit default swaps are beginning to shift. Credit spreads are widening as companies like Oracle increase leverage to raise funds for AI infrastructure. Equity volatility is no longer the only risk. Previously seemingly "unassailable" sectors are beginning to exhibit dynamic characteristics typical of credit cycles. Market structure amplifies this impact. These companies, with their excessive weighting in major indices, are shifting from "cash cows" to "leveraged growth" models, altering the overall risk profile of the indices. This does not automatically mean the end of the AI ​​"bubble." Debt-financed capital expenditures are justified if returns are genuine and sustainable. This does indeed mean a significant reduction in the margin for error, especially in an environment of high interest rates and tightening policies. 4. Early Cracks in Credit and the Private Market Beneath the surface of the public market, early signs of stress have emerged in private lending: the same loan is being valued significantly differently by different managers (e.g., some value it at 70% of face value, while others value it at around 90%). This divergence is a typical precursor to broader disputes between model valuations and market valuations. This pattern is similar to the following historical events: 2007 – Increased non-performing assets, widening interest rate spreads, while stock indices remained relatively calm. 2008 – Markets once considered cash equivalents (such as auction interest rate securities) suddenly froze. Furthermore, Federal Reserve reserves began to decline. There is a growing internal recognition that some form of balance sheet expansion may be needed to prevent problems in the functioning of the financial system. None of this means a crisis is inevitable. But it is consistent with a system where credit is quietly tightening, while policies still tout "data dependence" rather than preemptive measures. The repurchase market is the first signal that "reserve reserves are no longer sufficient." Zhou Shuheng and I recently discussed this point. In this radar chart, the "percentage of repurchase transactions with interest rates at or above the reserve balance rate" most clearly reflects that we are quietly withdrawing from a truly abundant reserve system. From the third quarter of 2018 to the beginning of 2019, this indicator remained relatively stable: ample reserves meant that most secured financing transactions were comfortably below the lower bound of the reserve balance rate. By September 2019, just before the repo market crash, this indicator surged, with more and more repo transactions reaching or exceeding the reserve balance rate—a typical symptom of collateral and reserve scarcity. Now, comparing June 2025 with October 2025: the light blue line (June) remains in a safe zone, but the red line for October 2025 has extended outwards, approaching the pattern of 2019, indicating that more and more repo transactions are hitting the lower bound of the policy rate. In other words, dealers and banks are raising overnight funding rates because reserves are no longer plentiful. Combined with other indicators (increased intraday overdrafts, increased federal funds purchases by U.S. depository institutions, and increased delayed payments), a clear signal emerges: the liquidity buffer in the financial system is weakening. 5. The K-shaped economy becomes a political variable. In my view, the economic divergence we have been calling "K-shaped" has now become a political variable: Household expectations are diverging. Long-term financial prospects (such as five-year expectations) show significant disparities: some groups expect stability or improvement, while others expect a sharp deterioration. Real-world stress indicators are flashing: subprime auto loan borrower default rates are rising. First-time homebuyer age is being delayed, with the median first-time homebuyer age approaching retirement age. Youth unemployment rates are rising slightly in several markets. For a growing number of people, the system is not only “unequal” but also dysfunctional: they lack assets, have limited wage growth, and virtually no real pathway to participate in asset inflation. The accepted social contract of "hard work, progress, wealth accumulation, and security" is crumbling. In this environment, political behavior is changing: voters no longer choose the "best managers" of the current system. They are increasingly willing to support candidates with disruptive or extreme stances on both the left and right, because for them, downside risk seems limited: "Things can't get any worse." Future policies regarding taxation, redistribution, regulation, and monetary support will be formulated within this context. This is not neutral for markets. 6. Market Concentration as a Systemic and Political Risk [Image of a PNG file] Market capitalization is concentrated in the hands of a few companies, yet its systemic and political implications are rarely discussed: Currently, the top ten companies account for approximately 40% of the weighting of major US stock indices. These companies are: core holdings of pension funds, 401(k) plans, and retail portfolios; increasingly reliant on artificial intelligence; exposed to risks in the Chinese market; and sensitive to interest rate paths. They effectively play a monopolistic role in multiple digital sectors. This raises three intertwined risks: **Systemic Market Risk:** Shocks to these companies—whether from profitability, regulation, or geopolitics (such as the Taiwan issue or changes in Chinese demand)—will quickly ripple through the entire household wealth system. **National Security Risk:** When a nation's vast wealth and productivity are concentrated in the hands of a few companies with external dependencies, they become strategic vulnerabilities. **Political Risk:** In an environment of K-shaped economies and rising populism, these companies become the most obvious focal points of discontent: higher taxes, windfall profits taxes, buyback restrictions, antitrust-based breakups, and stringent AI and data regulations. In other words, these companies are not only growth engines but also potential policy targets, and this possibility is rising. 7. Bitcoin, Gold, and the Temporary Failure of the "Perfect Hedging" Narrative In an environment of policy missteps, credit pressures, and political instability, one might expect Bitcoin to flourish as a macro hedge. However, the reality is: Gold exhibits characteristics of a traditional crisis hedge: steady strengthening, moderate volatility, and increasing importance in portfolios. Bitcoin's trading behavior is more like that of a high-beta risk asset: highly correlated with liquidity cycles. Sensitive to leverage and structured products. Early long-term holders are reducing their holdings in this environment. The initial decentralized/monetary revolution narrative remains conceptually attractive, but in practice: financialization is now the dominant driver of capital flows: yield strategies, derivatives, and low-volatility trading. Empirically, Bitcoin is closer to the beta coefficient of tech stocks than a neutral, robust hedge. I still believe that 2026 could be a major turning point for Bitcoin (the next policy cycle, the next round of stimulus, and further erosion of trust in traditional assets). However, investors should recognize that at this stage, Bitcoin does not offer the hedging properties many expect; it is part of the same liquidity system we are concerned about. 8. Feasible Scenario Framework for 2026 One effective way to understand the current environment is that it is a managed bubble tightening designed to create space for the next round of stimulus. Possible sequences are as follows: 1. 2024 to mid-2025: Controlled tightening and stress testing. Government shutdowns and political dysfunction cause cyclical drags. The Federal Reserve adopts a hawkish stance in rhetoric and balance sheet policy, tightening financial conditions. Credit spreads widen moderately; speculative sectors (artificial intelligence, long-duration tech stocks, and some private lending) bear the initial shock. 2. Late 2025 to 2026: Re-injection of liquidity under a political cycle. As inflation expectations decline and markets correct, policymakers regain easing "space." We will see interest rate cuts and fiscal measures to support growth and election goals. Due to policy lags, the inflationary consequences will manifest after key political junctures. 3. After 2026: System repricing. Depending on the size and form of the next round of stimulus, we will either face a new cycle of asset inflation, accompanied by more political and regulatory intervention, or more abruptly confront issues of debt sustainability, market concentration, and social contract. This framework is not deterministic, but it aligns with current incentives: politicians prioritize re-election over long-term equilibrium. The simplest tools remain liquidity injections and transfer payments, rather than structural reforms. To reuse these tools, they first need to deflate some of the current bubble. All signals point to the same conclusion: the system is entering a more fragile and less forgiving phase of this cycle. In fact, historical patterns suggest that policymakers will ultimately respond with massive liquidity injections. Entering the next phase requires navigating a period characterized by tightening financial conditions, increased credit sensitivity, heightened political volatility, and increasingly non-linear policy responses.
